title: The Military and the Republic
gist: Retired Air Force General Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie argue that America's founding-era anxiety about civilian control of the military is newly relevant: today's civilian political gridlock and high public trust in the armed forces create a 'reflex' to task the military with politically contentious domestic missions it should not perform, risking both mission focus and the military's nonpartisan standing.

insights.claim: The Declaration of Independence's list of grievances against King George III included that he 'affected to render the Military independent of and superior to the Civil power' — the founders' central fear was a military unaccountable to elected civilian authority.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: Frames the article's core lens: any modern policy question about domestic military deployment should be tested against this founding-era boundary between civil and military power.
insights.quote: affected to render the Military independent of and superior to the Civil power
insights.locator: In the Declaration of Independence, a chief complaint

insights.claim: George Washington deliberately deferred to the Continental Congress throughout the Revolutionary War despite its dysfunction, because he recognized that his authority over the Continental Army flowed from the people's elected representatives, not from his own popularity.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical precedent the authors use to judge modern presidents' use of the military — legitimacy derives from civilian political authority, not operational capability or popularity.
insights.quote: his authority over the Continental Army flowed from the people’s representatives
insights.locator: Throughout the Revolutionary War, he constantly deferred

themes.theme: Mission creep in the present day

insights.claim: Americans' high regard for the military relative to civilian government creates a political reflex to hand the armed forces problems too big for civilian institutions to solve, as both Trump and Biden did by giving the military a leading role in the COVID-19 pandemic response.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: Identifies a bipartisan, structural pattern (not a partisan one-off) of civilian institutions offloading hard problems onto the military — relevant to forecasting future domestic deployments regardless of which party holds the White House.
insights.locator: When the public is confronted with a problem

insights.claim: The U.S. military's overwhelming relative advantage — a budget two to three times that of its nearest competitor, and unique operational capability demonstrated by the capture of Venezuelan dictator Nicolàs Maduro — is precisely what makes the temptation toward domestic mission creep understandable.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the capability gap that policymakers can be tempted to misuse domestically; a useful marker for tracking future 'military as fix-all' policy moves.
insights.quote: a budget two to three times that of its nearest competitor
insights.locator: The temptation for mission creep is understandable

insights.claim: The authors warn that using the armed forces for politically contentious domestic missions such as addressing urban crime diverts the military from its primary combat mission and substitutes for fixing the underlying dysfunction in civilian institutions.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: A direct caution against normalizing domestic military deployment for law-and-order missions — a live policy debate with implications for defense readiness and domestic politics.
insights.locator: But when presidents use the armed forces for more politically contentious missions

insights.claim: The authors state plainly that overusing the military as a fix for political dysfunction risks 'the entire enterprise' of the republic, not just the specific mission at hand.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: The article's starkest warning — positions military overreach as a systemic, not incremental, risk to democratic institutions.
insights.quote: if you ask too much of the military, you risk the entire enterprise
insights.locator: And, as Washington knew, it is not the military’s job

insights.claim: Eisenhower's farewell address warned against the 'conjunction of an immense military establishment and a large arms industry,' cautioning that the country must never let the weight of that combination endanger its liberties or democratic processes.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: A historical checkpoint the authors use to argue that safeguarding the republic from an overweighted military-industrial nexus is a civilian, not military, responsibility.
insights.quote: We must never let the weight of this combination endanger our liberties or democratic processes.
insights.locator: In his farewell address, he warned about the risk

insights.claim: The military's outsize ceremonial role during the current 250th-anniversary commemorations (honor guards, parades, flyovers) is appropriate but should not be allowed to become a 'military monopoly on patriotism' that crowds out other forms of civilian national service.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: Signals the authors' concern that symbolic military prominence during the anniversary year could reinforce the same civil-military imbalance the article warns against, even absent any operational overreach.
insights.locator: But they should not be seen as

themes.theme: What the military owes the republic

insights.claim: The authors argue the military must stay humble and learn the lessons of the wars in Ukraine and Iran, explicitly including questioning traditional assumptions of U.S. air supremacy.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: A direct signal from a former Joint Chiefs Chairman that recent wars have undermined a core planning assumption (uncontested U.S. air dominance) — relevant to defense-sector capability and procurement debates.
insights.quote: must stay humble and learn the lessons of the wars in Ukraine and Iran, including questioning traditional assumptions of U.S. air supremacy
insights.locator: Indeed, despite its advantages, the U.S. military today

insights.claim: Sustaining the all-volunteer force requires finding new ways to keep the military connected to American society and to persuade capable young men and women from all walks of life to serve.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: Flags recruiting and civil-military social distance as an ongoing structural risk to force readiness, independent of any single conflict.
insights.quote: to persuade capable young men and women from all walks of life to serve
insights.locator: To maintain professionalism in its all-volunteer force

insights.claim: The military must protect the public trust it has earned by scrupulously following all lawful orders and demonstrating nonpartisanship every day, since service members swear an oath to the Constitution rather than to any party or leader.
insights.speaker: Charles Q. Brown, Jr., Peter D. Feaver, and Andrew Kragie
insights.so_what: The authors' bottom-line prescription for preserving institutional legitimacy — a benchmark for judging future controversies over politicization of the armed forces.
insights.quote: scrupulously following all lawful orders and by demonstrating every day that it is nonpartisan
insights.locator: And the military must protect the respect
